---
title: "02 · Matrices: Algebra, Inverse, and the Design Matrix"
subtitle: "A matrix is a grid of numbers — and the data structure every ML model is shaped by."
lesson-id: "mml.matrices"
tier: "mml"
prerequisites: ["mml.linear-systems", "t5.matrices"]
unlocks: ["mml.vector-spaces"]
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Before you start, you should be able to say *yes* to all four:

1. Can you state $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{x} = \boldsymbol{b}$ as a
   system of linear equations? — see
   [MML — 01, Linear Systems](../01-linear-systems/index.qmd).
2. Can you multiply two 2-by-2 matrices on paper? — see
   [Tier 5 — Matrices](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/01-matrices/index.qmd).
3. Can you state the dimensions rule: $\boldsymbol{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$
   times $\boldsymbol{B} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ gives what shape? — same prerequisite.
4. Do you know what a $2 \times 2$ identity matrix looks like?

If any answer is *no*, follow the link before continuing.
:::

## Touch

A spreadsheet is a matrix. Below is a tiny dataset: three students,
each with two test scores. Hover the cells; this is
$\boldsymbol{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 2}$ — three rows (one per
student), two columns (one per test).

```{ojs}
{
  const X = [[78, 91], [65, 70], [88, 84]];
  const rowLabels = ["Alice", "Bob", "Cara"];
  const colLabels = ["Test 1", "Test 2"];
  return html`<table style="border-collapse: collapse; font-family: sans-serif; margin-top: 8px;">
    <thead>
      <tr><th></th>${colLabels.map(c => `<th style="border:1px solid #ccc; padding:6px 14px; background:#f0f0f0">${c}</th>`).join("")}</tr>
    </thead>
    <tbody>
      ${X.map((row, i) => `<tr>
        <th style="border:1px solid #ccc; padding:6px 14px; background:#f0f0f0">${rowLabels[i]}</th>
        ${row.map(v => `<td style="border:1px solid #ccc; padding:6px 14px; text-align:right">${v}</td>`).join("")}
      </tr>`).join("")}
    </tbody>
  </table>`;
}
```

Now imagine we want to assign each test a weight — say
$\boldsymbol{w} = (0.4, 0.6)^\top$ — and get one weighted grade per
student. The operation that does this is **matrix–vector
multiplication**: $\boldsymbol{X}\boldsymbol{w}$ is a length-3 vector,
the weighted grade per student. Drag the slider for $w_1$ (Test 1's
weight); $w_2 = 1 - w_1$ adjusts automatically.

```{ojs}
viewof w1 = Inputs.range([0, 1], {value: 0.4, step: 0.01, label: "Test 1 weight w₁:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const X = [[78, 91], [65, 70], [88, 84]];
  const w = [w1, 1 - w1];
  const grades = X.map(row => row[0]*w[0] + row[1]*w[1]);
  const rowLabels = ["Alice", "Bob", "Cara"];
  return html`<table style="border-collapse: collapse; font-family: sans-serif; margin-top:6px;">
    <thead><tr><th></th><th style="padding:6px 14px;background:#f0f0f0;border:1px solid #ccc;">Grade = X w</th></tr></thead>
    <tbody>
      ${grades.map((g,i) => `<tr>
        <th style="padding:6px 14px;background:#f0f0f0;border:1px solid #ccc">${rowLabels[i]}</th>
        <td style="padding:6px 14px;border:1px solid #ccc;text-align:right">${g.toFixed(1)}</td>
      </tr>`).join("")}
    </tbody>
  </table>`;
}
```

The matrix $\boldsymbol{X}$ holds the data. The vector $\boldsymbol{w}$
holds the model parameters. The product $\boldsymbol{X}\boldsymbol{w}$
holds the predictions. *That is linear regression in one sentence.*

## Wonder

1. Why does the rule "rows of the first times columns of the second"
   for matrix multiplication require the *inner* dimensions to match,
   not the outer?
2. If $\boldsymbol{X}$ has 3 rows and 2 columns, what shape is
   $\boldsymbol{X}\boldsymbol{w}$? Three numbers, two, six? Why?
3. We chose weights $w_1$ and $w_2$ that sum to 1. Why might that
   make $\boldsymbol{X}\boldsymbol{w}$ feel like an *average* of the
   columns?
4. Could there be a matrix $\boldsymbol{Y}$ such that $\boldsymbol{Y}\boldsymbol{X}$
   gives a length-2 vector? What would its shape need to be?

## Discover

Six problems that build matrix algebra from $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{x} = \boldsymbol{b}$.

**D1.** Compute $\boldsymbol{X}\boldsymbol{w}$ by hand where
$\boldsymbol{X} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 3 \\ 4 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$
and $\boldsymbol{w} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2,\ 0 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 2,\ 4 \cdot 1 + (-1) \cdot 2)^\top = (4, 6, 2)^\top$.
Three rows of $\boldsymbol{X}$ produce three components in
$\boldsymbol{X}\boldsymbol{w}$.
</details>

**D2.** Verify by direct computation that
$(\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B})\boldsymbol{C} = \boldsymbol{A}(\boldsymbol{B}\boldsymbol{C})$
for $\boldsymbol{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$,
$\boldsymbol{B} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$,
$\boldsymbol{C} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\boldsymbol{B}\boldsymbol{C} = (5, 3)^\top$, so $\boldsymbol{A}(\boldsymbol{B}\boldsymbol{C}) = (5, 6)^\top$.
$\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$,
so $(\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B})\boldsymbol{C} = (5, 6)^\top$. Equal.
Matrix multiplication is *associative* — but you have to compute the
sizes to even check this.
</details>

**D3.** Find a $2 \times 2$ matrix $\boldsymbol{A}$ and another
$\boldsymbol{B}$ such that $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B} \ne \boldsymbol{B}\boldsymbol{A}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Try $\boldsymbol{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$,
$\boldsymbol{B} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$,
$\boldsymbol{B}\boldsymbol{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$.
Not equal. **Matrix multiplication is *not* commutative** — and this
is why composing two linear maps depends on order.
</details>

**D4.** Find a $2 \times 2$ matrix $\boldsymbol{I}$ such that
$\boldsymbol{I}\boldsymbol{A} = \boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{I} = \boldsymbol{A}$
for every $2 \times 2$ matrix $\boldsymbol{A}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\boldsymbol{I} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ —
the **identity matrix**. Plays the role of $1$ in ordinary
multiplication. There is one for each square size: $\boldsymbol{I}_n$ for $n \times n$.
</details>

**D5.** Suppose $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B} = \boldsymbol{I}$ for
some square $\boldsymbol{B}$. Does that mean $\boldsymbol{B}\boldsymbol{A} = \boldsymbol{I}$
too? Try
$\boldsymbol{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$,
$\boldsymbol{B} = \frac{1}{-2}\begin{pmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -3 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Compute: $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B} = \boldsymbol{I}$ and
$\boldsymbol{B}\boldsymbol{A} = \boldsymbol{I}$ as well. For
*square* matrices, one-sided inverses are two-sided inverses
(a deep theorem). We call $\boldsymbol{B}$ the **inverse** of
$\boldsymbol{A}$ and write $\boldsymbol{B} = \boldsymbol{A}^{-1}$.
Not every square matrix has one — only those with nonzero determinant.
</details>

**D6.** Given $\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}$ exists, give a one-line formula
for the solution of $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{x} = \boldsymbol{b}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Multiply both sides on the left by $\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}$:
$\boldsymbol{x} = \boldsymbol{A}^{-1}\boldsymbol{b}$. *Beautiful in
theory — but in practice we never actually compute $\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}$;
we do Gaussian elimination directly because it's $\sim 3\times$ faster
and numerically more stable.*
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The English mathematician **Arthur Cayley** wrote the first paper on
the algebra of matrices in 1858 (*A Memoir on the Theory of Matrices*),
proving among other things that every square matrix satisfies its own
characteristic polynomial — the **Cayley–Hamilton theorem**. He did
this before *vectors* were a standard idea. The "matrix" had to come
first; vectors as algebra-friendly objects came later.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A matrix is *two things at once*: a **data table** (your dataset,
your design matrix $\boldsymbol{X}$) and a **transformation** (the
operation $\boldsymbol{x} \mapsto \boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{x}$ that
sends one vector to another). Every confusion about matrices comes
from losing track of which view you're in.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}
A **matrix** $\boldsymbol{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is a
rectangular array with $m$ rows and $n$ columns. Its $(i, j)$ entry
is $a_{ij}$.

The **matrix–matrix product** of
$\boldsymbol{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ and
$\boldsymbol{B} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ is the matrix
$\boldsymbol{C} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times p}$ with entries
$$
c_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^n a_{ik} b_{kj}.
$$

The **identity matrix** $\boldsymbol{I}_n \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$
has $1$ on the diagonal and $0$ elsewhere. The **inverse** of a
square $\boldsymbol{A}$, when it exists, is the unique
$\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}$ with $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{A}^{-1} = \boldsymbol{A}^{-1}\boldsymbol{A} = \boldsymbol{I}$.

The **design matrix** of a dataset with $N$ examples and $D$ features
is $\boldsymbol{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times D}$ — one row per example,
one column per feature. This is the matrix every supervised-learning
algorithm in this track starts with.

::: {.callout-warning}
**Sūtra.** *Rows by columns, inner dimensions match — and order is
not negotiable.*
:::
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
**Watch out:**

1. *"Matrix multiplication is element-wise."* — Almost never. The
   element-wise (Hadamard) product exists but is rare. $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B}$
   is the rows-by-columns inner-product rule above.
2. *"$\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}$ always exists if $\boldsymbol{A}$ is square."* —
   Wrong. It exists iff $\det \boldsymbol{A} \ne 0$. The all-ones
   matrix is square but has no inverse.
3. *"Computing $\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}\boldsymbol{b}$ is the way to solve
   $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{x} = \boldsymbol{b}$."* — Mathematically
   yes, numerically no. NumPy's `solve` uses LU decomposition; the
   inverse is reserved for theoretical work.
:::

## Apply

### Worked example

A linear regression on the dataset
$\boldsymbol{X} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$,
$\boldsymbol{y} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \\ 6 \end{pmatrix}$ — the
first column of $\boldsymbol{X}$ is the intercept, the second is the
feature. The **normal equations** for the least-squares fit are
$\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{w} = \boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{y}$.

**Step 1 — Form $\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X}$:**
$\boldsymbol{X}^\top = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$.
Then $\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 10 \\ 10 & 38 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Step 2 — Form $\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{y}$:**
$\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{y} = \begin{pmatrix} 13 \\ 48 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Step 3 — Solve the $2 \times 2$ system** (any way you like — use
Lesson 01's elimination). Multiplying through:
$3 w_0 + 10 w_1 = 13$, $10 w_0 + 38 w_1 = 48$. Eliminating $w_0$
gives $w_1 = (48 - \tfrac{10}{3} \cdot 13)/(38 - \tfrac{100}{3}) = (48 - 43.\overline{3})/4.\overline{6} = 1$ exactly. Then $w_0 = (13 - 10)/3 = 1$.

**Answer:** $\boldsymbol{w} = (1, 1)^\top$, i.e. the regression line
is $y = 1 + 1 \cdot x_1$. We just *did* linear regression with two
matrix products and one $2 \times 2$ system.

### Warm

**W1.** Compute $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B}$ for
$\boldsymbol{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$,
$\boldsymbol{B} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 4 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$. (Right-multiplying by
the swap matrix $\boldsymbol{B}$ swaps the columns of $\boldsymbol{A}$.)
</details>

**W2.** What is the inverse of $\boldsymbol{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\begin{pmatrix} 1/2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1/5 \end{pmatrix}$. The inverse of a
diagonal matrix is the diagonal of reciprocals.
</details>

**W3.** Given $\boldsymbol{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{100 \times 5}$ — a
dataset of 100 examples and 5 features — what are the shapes of
$\boldsymbol{X}^\top$, $\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X}$, and
$\boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{X}^\top$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\boldsymbol{X}^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{5 \times 100}$,
$\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{5 \times 5}$,
$\boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{X}^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{100 \times 100}$.
The two products are *very* different sizes — and ML algorithms
choose between them depending on whether $N < D$ or $D < N$. We'll see
this trick in PCA (Lesson 27).
</details>

### Working

**Wo1.** Show $(\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B})^\top = \boldsymbol{B}^\top \boldsymbol{A}^\top$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$[(\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B})^\top]_{ij} = (\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B})_{ji} = \sum_k a_{jk} b_{ki}$.
$[\boldsymbol{B}^\top \boldsymbol{A}^\top]_{ij} = \sum_k (\boldsymbol{B}^\top)_{ik} (\boldsymbol{A}^\top)_{kj} = \sum_k b_{ki} a_{jk}$.
Same sum. (Note the *reversal* — this is why the normal equations are
$\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X}$, not $\boldsymbol{X}\boldsymbol{X}^\top$.)
</details>

**Wo2.** Suppose $\boldsymbol{A}$ and $\boldsymbol{B}$ are both
invertible $n \times n$ matrices. Prove that
$(\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B})^{-1} = \boldsymbol{B}^{-1}\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B})(\boldsymbol{B}^{-1}\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}) = \boldsymbol{A}(\boldsymbol{B}\boldsymbol{B}^{-1})\boldsymbol{A}^{-1} = \boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{A}^{-1} = \boldsymbol{I}$.
By uniqueness of inverses, this is the inverse. *Reverse the order
when inverting a product* — the "socks and shoes" rule.
</details>

**Wo3.** Express the operation "stack $\boldsymbol{X}$'s columns into
a single column vector" as multiplication by some matrix — or argue
that you can't.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
You *can* — the **vectorisation** operator $\mathrm{vec}(\cdot)$ is a
linear map, so it has a matrix representation (via Kronecker products).
But it's neither left- nor right-multiplication by a single matrix.
This is one of the cases where you need a more general linear map than
plain matrix multiplication. We'll meet linear maps in Lesson 05.
</details>

### Stretch

**S1.** For a square matrix $\boldsymbol{A}$, the **trace** is
$\mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{A}) = \sum_i a_{ii}$. Show that
$\mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B}) = \mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{B}\boldsymbol{A})$
whenever both products are defined and square.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Write out $\mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B}) = \sum_i (\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B})_{ii}$
as a double sum.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B}) = \sum_i \sum_k a_{ik} b_{ki} = \sum_k \sum_i b_{ki} a_{ik} = \mathrm{tr}(\boldsymbol{B}\boldsymbol{A})$.
Despite the non-commutativity of multiplication, the *trace* of
$\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B}$ doesn't care about order. We'll use
this constantly when computing gradients of $\mathrm{tr}$-expressions
in Lesson 15.
</details>

**S2.** A *one-hot* encoding turns a categorical label into a length-$K$
vector with a single 1. If $\boldsymbol{X} \in \{0, 1\}^{N \times K}$
is a stack of $N$ one-hot rows, what does
$\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X}$ represent?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X})_{kk'} = \sum_n x_{nk} x_{nk'}$.
Since each row has a single $1$, the only contributors are rows where
*both* $x_{nk}$ and $x_{nk'}$ are $1$ — which forces $k = k'$. So
$\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X}$ is diagonal with entry
$\#\{n : x_{nk} = 1\}$ — the **count** of each category. A one-line
matrix product gives you a class-count histogram.
</details>

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A matrix is both a data table and a transformation — keep both pictures in mind.**
2. **Matrix multiplication is rows-by-columns, inner dimensions must match, and order matters.**
3. **In ML, the design matrix $\boldsymbol{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times D}$ is where every supervised problem starts — and $\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X}$ is where most of them lead.**

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Can you state the matrix-multiplication rule and the dimensions
   check in one breath, to a friend?
2. Did you solve at least 6 of the 9 practice problems without
   peeking at hints?
3. When you see $\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X}$ in a paper, do
   you immediately see it as a Gram matrix / a covariance proxy?

- **3 yes:** move on to Lesson 03.
- **2 yes:** redo Wo1 and Wo2 from scratch, then move on.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* and the worked example; the dual
  picture (data vs. transformation) hasn't landed yet.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`mml.linear-systems`](../01-linear-systems/index.qmd) — gave us
  $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{x} = \boldsymbol{b}$ as the motivation
  for studying matrices.
- [`t5.matrices`](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/01-matrices/index.qmd) —
  the synthesis-tier pure-linear-algebra view of matrix multiplication.

### What this unlocks

- [`mml.vector-spaces`](../03-vector-spaces/index.qmd) — the structure
  the columns of a matrix actually live in.
- Eventually, [`mml.linear-regression-mle`](../24-linear-regression-mle/index.qmd) —
  where the normal equations $\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{w} = \boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{y}$ are derived from first principles.

### Sister synthesis lesson

[`t5.matrices`](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/01-matrices/index.qmd) is
the pure-LA view. Read it for the algebraic rules; read *this* lesson
for the design-matrix and ML-pipeline framing.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** The matrix product $\boldsymbol{X}\boldsymbol{w}$ is the
forward pass of a linear regression *and* the forward pass of a single
neural-network layer (followed by a non-linearity). Modern deep
learning is, at every layer, a matrix multiplication.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Databases.** Joins on a relational schema can often be written as
matrix products of incidence matrices. Sparse-matrix multiplication
algorithms (CSR, CSC formats) are how query engines run on TPUs/GPUs.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Systems.** GPUs are matrix-multiplication accelerators —
the `tensor core` instruction performs a $4 \times 4 \times 4$ matrix
multiply-and-accumulate as a single hardware op. ML workloads scale
with the speed of this one operation.
:::
